title: Wide Bookcase Base — Modular Office Collection
summary: A shallow, wide plywood bookcase with double top and bottom trim — sized to serve as a desk base or stand on its own.
description:
This bookcase is deliberately shallow and wide rather than tall — 15-1/4" deep — which is what makes it work as a desk base as easily as a bookcase; a shallower box gives you legroom underneath if you set a desktop across two of these. The double-thickness trim at the top and bottom (a 1x2 layered with a 1x3) is what gives the edges enough visual weight that the case doesn't read as a plain plywood box, and it's applied inside the front opening as well as around the outside.

Rip your whole sheet of plywood to 15-1/4" wide before cutting anything to length — every solid panel in this case shares that depth, so getting the rip consistent up front saves you from chasing small width mismatches between parts later.

materials.name: 3/4" MDF or A1 plywood, ripped 15-1/4" wide, 8 ft sheet

steps.title: Rip and cut the parts
steps.body:
Rip the whole 3/4" sheet to 15-1/4" wide before cutting anything to length. The top, both sides and both shelves all share that depth, so one consistent rip setting is what stops you chasing 1/16" mismatches at assembly.

Crosscut from those strips: the top at 40", two sides at 29", two shelves at 38-1/2" (40" less the two 3/4" side thicknesses). Cut the 1/4" back at 29-3/4" x 40" — that is the case's exact outside face, the 29" sides plus the 3/4" top. From the 1x2, cut the inside front trim at 38-1/2", two side trims at 15-1/2" and the front trim at 41-1/2"; cut the same three lengths from the 1x3 for the bottom trim. The 15-1/2" pieces are the case depth plus the back, and the 41-1/2" pieces are the case width plus the two 3/4" side trims — so if your rip drifts, re-measure the assembled case and cut the trim to it instead.
materials: 3/4" MDF or A1 plywood, ripped 15-1/4" wide, 8 ft sheet

steps.title: Build the box
steps.body:
Stand the two 29" sides on edge and set the two 38-1/2" shelves between them — one flush with the bottom ends, the other at whatever height you want the single shelf, since the plan does not fix it. Cap them with the 40" top so it sits across the side ends and the case reads a full 40" wide.

Fasten with 2" self-tapping screws driven through the sides into the shelf edges, adding glue at every joint; 3/4" pocket holes with pocket screws work just as well if you would rather hide the fasteners. Compare the diagonals before the last screws go tight — a racked case makes the 1/4" back fight you in the next step, and by then it is too late to fix.
materials: 3/4" MDF or A1 plywood, ripped 15-1/4" wide, 8 ft sheet

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body:
Run a bead of glue along the back edges of the sides, shelves and top, lay the 29-3/4" x 40" panel on, and line one corner up flush. The back is cut to the case's exact outside dimensions, which makes it your squaring tool as well as the back — pull the case into line with the panel's edges before a single nail goes in.

Fix it with 1-1/4" brad nails spaced evenly around the perimeter and along each shelf edge, so no part of the panel can lift and buzz later.

steps.title: Add the inside front trim
steps.body:
Fit the two 38-1/2" pieces inside the front opening — the 1x2 up under the top and the 1x3 across the front of the bottom shelf — so they close off the plywood edges you can see into. Glue them, then pin through the sides and shelves into the ends and edges of the trim with 1-1/4" brad nails; nailing from the case into the trim keeps every hole off the show face.

Check each piece sits flush with the front edges of the sides before the glue grabs. Trim left proud here will telegraph through the outer trim you add next, and there is no sanding it back once it is layered.

steps.title: Add the side and outer front trim, then finish
steps.body:
Glue and pin the two 15-1/2" 1x2 and two 15-1/2" 1x3 pieces to the front edges of the sides, 1x2 at the top and 1x3 at the bottom, keeping the layered 1x2-over-1x3 arrangement the same all the way round. Then run the 41-1/2" front pieces across, overlapping the ends of the side trim so the joint reads as one continuous band — that overlap is exactly why the front pieces are 1-1/2" longer than the 40" case.

Fill every nail hole with wood filler, let it harden, sand flush and finish. Sand the top surface last and check nothing stands proud, since this case is meant to carry a desktop across it.

images.alt: Wide, shallow plywood bookcase base for a modular office desk
